retargeting audience where there's a pretty hard cap on how big it is, increasing the budget there won't really increase reach, but it will dramatically increase frequency, right? Totally, yeah. It's like I talked about in the retargeting module, there's really no scaling of a retargeting campaign. And so it's, I need a little bit more information to answer it, but I can give you a good rule of thumb. So bounce rate is basically telling you what percentage of people visit your page, do nothing and then leave, right? So if, you know, people go to your page and they don't click any links and they don't go to any more pages and they leave, that's a bounce. But if you have things like video on your page, you don't necessarily know if they're watching the video or not, if you don't have events set up. So I would say if someone goes to your page, watches the video and leaves, that wouldn't call that a bounce, right? Because they engage, they interact, they watch some of your content. And so it kind of depends on how you have your site set up. If you're tracking events or people spending a certain amount of time on the page or events for people playing videos or scrolling more than 50% of the way through the page, tracking those events, your bounce rate should be probably, ah, it's kind of a good rule of thumb, like below 30 would be my goal. If you're not tracking any of those events, so you're not tracking video, scroll, or time on page as an event, and I'm specifically using the event with capital E, right, how Google Analytics uses those, then if your bounce rate is below 75, it's not terrible. You know, obviously the less people bounce, the more likely they are to buy and do all that other stuff. But sometimes the page itself, right, is the thing doing the job. Great question. This is very natural. It just happened over Mother's Day two, and there are a few ways to look at it. If that doesn't make sense for you. So for example, during December at Digital Marketer, John, we used to turn pretty much all ads off because we knew that our audience of entrepreneurs and marketers were checking out to spend time with their family. They were worried about Best Buy's big sale that was happening, not us. So there are a few ways. It really depends on your business and who you're serving. Another way to think about this, if you have evergreen campaigns running that are working, I always just bite the bullet and keep them running and spend more during a big holiday time because I don't want to turn it off and then take the risk of trying to turn it back on after the holiday and it not perform like it had, and seeing a drop in results.
